title: Meta Accounts for Wearables
role:
Senior Product Designer
Identity & Account Lead
team: Design · Research · Identity Engineering · Product · Legal
credits:
Meta Reality Labs — Identity
PM, Research, Engineering, and the Meta Identity team
brief: Designed the migration from Facebook accounts to Meta accounts across the wearables ecosystem. Touched tens of millions of users worldwide, navigated complex account-linking flows, and made sure people always understood what was happening to their data at every step.

body.heading: Problem
body.body:
How do you migrate millions of users from one identity system to another without losing trust, breaking sessions, or leaving anyone in a half-migrated state on the device they wear on their face?

Facebook accounts had been the front door to wearables since Ray-Ban Stories. As Meta's identity platform evolved, the company needed every wearable user to land on a Meta account — but on their schedule, with full control, and without risk of losing access to their existing photos, settings, and paired devices.

body.heading: Research
body.body:
Six weeks of qualitative interviews with 40+ Ray-Ban Meta users plus two large quantitative studies on intent-to-migrate language and consent-flow comprehension.

The biggest insight: users didn't care about the account system change. They cared about three things — "will my photos be safe," "will my glasses still pair," and "can I undo this." The migration UX needed to answer those three questions in the first three screens, every time.

media.alt: Account-link consent flow on a paired phone.

body.heading: Solution
body.body:
Progressive disclosure of complexity. Each screen does one thing well: introduce the change, confirm what carries over, get the consent, and verify the result.

A central "migration receipt" screen captures everything that moved (photos, paired devices, payment methods, friends list when applicable) and gives the user a printable / emailable summary. This single artifact dropped support contact volume by an order of magnitude in the first market.

A dedicated "undo" surface in the first 30 days post-migration meant the system could absorb mistakes — which gave Legal and Trust the confidence to ship the migration as opt-in defaults rather than gated.

media.alt: 30-day undo surface in the companion app settings.
Three Ray-Ban Meta phones showing the new Meta account login flow for wearables
media.caption: The unified Meta account sign-in across Ray-Ban Meta and the View companion app.

body.heading: Reflection
body.body: Account migrations are 80% trust and 20% UX. The pixels matter, but what really moved the metric was the team's collective discipline about being honest with users — naming what was changing, what wasn't, and what they could undo if they changed their mind. That's the work the design system can't do for you.
